transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Blaise. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I’m not really sure. There’s a lot going on, but I don’t know if it’s worth talking about. I mean, it’s just... family stuff, I suppose. It’s complicated, and I don’t want to make things worse. I usually just keep it to myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a bit overwhelmed with family issues, and there’s a sense of hesitation about sharing because you’re concerned it might complicate things further. That’s completely understandable. Can you tell me a little more about what’s been on your mind regarding your family?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, it’s just... sometimes I feel like I’m stuck in the middle. Like, if I say something, it might upset someone, especially my dad. I don’t want to lose his support or make him feel torn between me and the rest of the family. So, I usually just keep quiet or try to smooth things over, but it doesn’t always work out. It’s just... stressful, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of pressure to maintain harmony within your family, especially when it comes to your dad. Being in the middle can be really challenging, and it makes sense that you’d want to avoid upsetting anyone. You mentioned that keeping quiet or smoothing things over doesn’t always work—can you share more about what happens when it doesn’t?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, well, when it doesn’t work, I just feel... tense, I guess. Like, I might try to express how I feel, but then it just leads to more conflict. I end up apologizing a lot, even if I don’t think I did anything wrong. I just want to avoid any kind of argument or disagreement. It’s like I can’t handle the thought of everyone being upset, especially my dad. So I just... back down. It’s easier that way, I think.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you experience a lot of tension when you try to express your feelings, and that often leads to conflict, which you want to avoid at all costs. Apologizing, even when you don’t feel it’s warranted, seems to be a way for you to keep the peace. I can see how that might feel easier in the moment, but it also sounds quite exhausting for you. How do you feel about backing down in those situations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it is exhausting. I mean, part of me feels relieved when I back down because it stops the immediate tension, but then I also feel... frustrated, I guess? Like I’m not really being heard or understood. It’s like I’m just going along with whatever to keep things calm, but deep down, I wish I could just say what I really think without worrying about the fallout. But then I think about how my dad might react, and it just makes me anxious again. So, I don’t know. It’s a bit of a cycle, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re caught in a cycle where backing down brings you temporary relief, but it also leaves you feeling frustrated and unheard. The anxiety about how your dad might react seems to weigh heavily on you, making it difficult to express your true thoughts. It’s understandable to feel torn between wanting to maintain peace and the desire to be authentic. What do you think holds you back the most from expressing yourself openly?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s mostly the fear of disappointing my dad or making him feel like he has to choose sides. I don’t want to create any tension or make things worse than they already are. I’ve seen how family disagreements can escalate, and I just... I don’t want to be the reason for that. So I guess I just keep my thoughts to myself. It feels safer, even if it’s not really what I want.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like your fear of disappointing your dad and the potential for escalating family disagreements really drives your decision to keep your thoughts to yourself. You’ve witnessed how conflicts can grow, and that experience seems to have made you very cautious about speaking up. It’s understandable to want to protect your family dynamics, even at the cost of your own feelings. How does it feel for you to prioritize safety in this way, even if it means holding back your true thoughts?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It feels... heavy, I guess. Like I’m carrying this weight around all the time. I know I’m trying to keep things safe, but it also feels like I’m not being true to myself. I want to be able to share what I think without worrying about the consequences, but I just can’t shake that feeling of anxiety. It’s like I’m stuck between wanting to be honest and wanting to keep the peace. It’s frustrating, and sometimes I wonder if it’s worth it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a significant burden from trying to balance honesty with the desire to keep the peace. That heaviness you describe seems to come from the conflict between your true self and the role you feel you need to play in your family. It’s understandable to question whether this approach is worth it, especially when it leads to frustration and anxiety. What do you think might happen if you were able to express yourself more freely, even just a little?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I don’t know... I guess part of me thinks it could be freeing, like maybe I wouldn’t feel so trapped all the time. But then there’s that nagging fear of how my dad might react. What if he doesn’t understand or gets upset? I worry it could lead to more problems, and I really don’t want that. But at the same time, I think maybe if I could just share a bit, it might help him see where I’m coming from. It’s just hard to imagine actually doing it without feeling that anxiety creeping back in.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there’s a part of you that recognizes the potential for freedom and relief in expressing yourself, yet the fear of your dad’s reaction holds you back. It’s a tough position to be in—wanting to share your perspective while also feeling anxious about the possible fallout. The idea that sharing even a little might help him understand you better is an important insight. What do you think would help you feel more comfortable taking that first step toward sharing your thoughts?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe if I had a clearer idea of how he might react, it would help. Like, if I knew he’d be open to listening without getting defensive, I might feel more at ease. Or maybe if I could practice what I want to say beforehand, it wouldn’t feel so daunting. But it’s hard to know how to approach it without feeling like I’m walking on eggshells. I just wish it could be easier to talk about these things without all the worry.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that having a clearer sense of how your dad might react would help ease your anxiety. Practicing what you want to say could also give you more confidence. It sounds like you’re looking for a way to navigate this conversation that feels safer and less fraught with tension. The desire for open communication without the worry of walking on eggshells is completely valid. What do you think would be the first small step you could take toward having that conversation with him?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could start by just bringing up something small about how I feel, something that isn’t too controversial. Like, just sharing a little bit about my perspective on a family event or something. If he responds well to that, it might give me the confidence to go a bit deeper later on. I think starting small could help me gauge his reaction without diving into the heavier stuff right away. It feels less intimidating that way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach. Starting with something small and less controversial could help you ease into the conversation and gauge your dad's reaction without feeling overwhelmed. It’s great that you’re considering a way to build your confidence gradually. How do you feel about identifying a specific family event or situation you could use as a starting point?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I could think about the last family gathering we had. There were some things that happened that I didn’t really agree with, but I didn’t say anything at the time. Maybe I could mention how I felt about that in a casual way. It’s not too heavy, and it might open the door for a broader conversation later. I just have to remind myself to keep it light and not overthink it too much.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan. Reflecting on the last family gathering and sharing your feelings in a casual way could be a good entry point for conversation. It’s understandable to want to keep it light and not overthink it, especially as you’re starting this process. How do you feel about the idea of approaching your dad with this perspective? What emotions come up for you as you think about it?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of things, honestly. There’s a bit of hope that maybe it could go well, but also a lot of anxiety. I worry about how he might react, and if he’ll really listen or just brush it off. It’s nerve-wracking to think about putting myself out there like that. But at the same time, I think it could be a relief to finally say something instead of just holding it in. So, I guess it’s a bit of a balancing act between hope and fear right now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely natural to feel that mix of hope and anxiety as you consider taking this step. The potential relief of expressing yourself is exciting, but the fear of how your dad might respond is understandably nerve-wracking. It sounds like you’re aware of the balancing act between wanting to be heard and the fear of vulnerability. As you prepare for this conversation, what do you think might help you manage that anxiety and stay grounded in the moment?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe taking a few deep breaths before I talk to him could help. Just to calm my nerves a bit. And maybe reminding myself that it’s okay if the conversation doesn’t go perfectly. I could also try to focus on what I want to say rather than getting too caught up in how he might react. It might help to have a few key points in mind so I don’t feel lost in the moment. Just trying to stay present and not let my mind spiral into worst-case scenarios could make a difference.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like excellent strategies. Taking deep breaths can really help center you, and reminding yourself that it’s okay if the conversation doesn’t go perfectly is a healthy mindset. Focusing on your key points and staying present can also help you navigate the conversation more effectively. It’s great that you’re thinking about how to manage your anxiety in a proactive way. How do you feel about setting a timeline for when you might try to have this conversation with your dad?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Setting a timeline could be helpful, I think. It gives me something to work toward instead of just putting it off indefinitely. Maybe I could aim for sometime in the next week or so? That way, I have a bit of time to prepare but not so much that I overthink it. I just need to find a moment when it feels right, you know? But having a target in mind might help me feel more committed to actually doing it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a great plan. Aiming for sometime in the next week gives you a clear target while still allowing you time to prepare. It’s wise to consider finding the right moment, as that can make a difference in how the conversation unfolds. Having that commitment can also provide motivation and focus. As you think about this timeline, how do you feel about the idea of checking in with yourself as the date approaches? What might that look like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in with myself as the date approaches could be really beneficial. It would give me a chance to assess how I’m feeling and if I need to adjust anything in my preparation. Maybe I could set aside a few moments each day to reflect on my thoughts and emotions about the conversation. I could jot down any worries or things I want to remember to say. Just taking that time to check in might help me feel more grounded and ready when the time comes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach. Setting aside time each day to reflect on your thoughts and emotions can help you process any worries and clarify what you want to communicate. Jotting down your thoughts can also serve as a helpful reminder when the time comes. It’s great that you’re prioritizing self-reflection and grounding yourself in this process. How do you feel about the support you might need from yourself or others as you prepare for this conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could use some support, especially from myself. Just being kind to myself and reminding myself that it’s okay to feel anxious would be helpful. I might also consider talking to a close friend about it, just to get some encouragement and maybe even practice what I want to say. Having someone to bounce ideas off of could make me feel less alone in this. It’s nice to think that I don’t have to go through it all by myself.
